title: Pipes and Redirection
description:
## Reading a Tricky Command

A deployment script runs a build step and you want to **watch all of its output live on screen** while **also saving everything — including error messages — to a log file**. A colleague suggests:

```bash
./build.sh 2>&1 | tee build.log
```

What does this command actually do?

options.text: It merges stderr into stdout (2>&1), then pipes that combined stream to tee, which writes it to build.log and also prints it to the terminal.
It sends only stderr to build.log and discards stdout entirely.
It pipes stdout to tee but leaves stderr going only to the terminal, so error lines never reach build.log.
2>&1 redirects stdout into stderr, so build.log ends up empty.

explanation: Break it into pieces. `2>&1` means "redirect **file descriptor 2 (stderr)** to wherever **file descriptor 1 (stdout)** currently points" — so error output is **merged into the stdout stream**. The pipe `|` then sends that combined stream to **`tee`**, a tool that writes its input to a file (`build.log`) *and* passes it through to its own stdout (the terminal) — giving you a live view **and** a saved log of everything. Option b is wrong: nothing here sends *only* stderr to the file or discards stdout. Option c describes what would happen *without* the `2>&1` (errors would bypass the pipe), which is exactly why the merge is added. Option d misreads the direction: `2>&1` points stderr at stdout's destination, it does not redirect stdout into stderr. (Reminder: `>` overwrites stdout to a file, `>>` appends, and `2>` redirects stderr to a file.)

hints.title: Decode each token
hints.body: Read it as three parts: '2>&1' (merge stderr into stdout), '|' (pipe the combined stream onward), 'tee FILE' (write to FILE and echo to the screen).
